sections.heading: Why does English have silent letters?
sections.body: English spelling was largely fixed in the 15th century, but pronunciation kept evolving. Silent letters are relics ‚ÄĒ sounds that were once pronounced but disappeared over centuries of language change.

sections.heading: Common silent letter patterns
sections.body: **Silent K:** always before 'n' at the start of a word (know, knife, kneel, knock). **Silent W:** before 'r' at the start of a word (write, wrong, wrap, wrist). **Silent G:** before 'n' at the start or end (gnat, gnaw, sign, foreign). **Silent B:** after 'm' at the end (bomb, comb, lamb, thumb). **Silent H:** in many common words (hour, honest, heir, ghost).

sections.heading: Silent letters in the middle
sections.body: Many common words have silent letters in the middle that trip up even advanced learners: 'Wednesday' /ňąw…õnzde…™/, 'February' /ňąf…õbru…õri/ ‚Üí often /ňąf…õbju…õri/, 'comfortable' /ňąk Ćmft…ôb…ôl/, 'vegetable' /ňąv…õd ít…ôb…ôl/.

exercises.instruction: Cross out the silent letter(s) in each word.

quiz.question: Which word has a silent 'p'?
quiz.explanation: 'Pneumonia' is pronounced /njuňźňąmo äni…ô/ ‚ÄĒ the P is completely silent.

quiz.question: The word 'castle' is pronounced:
quiz.explanation: The T in 'castle' is silent: /ňąk√¶s…ôl/ (British English).
